title: Common Indian English Mistakes - 50 Mistakes
definition: Certain phrases and structures are extremely common in everyday Indian English but are considered non-standard in international/formal English (used in exams, business writing, and global communication). Here are 50 of the most common ones, explained simply, with the internationally accepted alternative.

commonMistakes.wrong: I am having two brothers.
commonMistakes.right: I have two brothers.
commonMistakes.why: 'Have' showing possession or family relationships is a state, not an action, so it should never be used in continuous ('-ing') form. This is one of the most common Indian English patterns - remember: possession and relationships use simple 'have/has', never 'am/is/are having'.

commonMistakes.wrong: Do the needful.
commonMistakes.right: Please take the necessary action. / Please handle this.
commonMistakes.why: "Do the needful" is a phrase specific to Indian English that sounds outdated or unclear to international readers - it's better to state exactly what action you want taken. Replace vague, formal-sounding Indian English phrases with clear, specific requests in international business writing.

commonMistakes.wrong: Revert back to me by tomorrow.
commonMistakes.right: Please get back to me by tomorrow. / Please reply by tomorrow.
commonMistakes.why: 'Revert' by itself already means 'to return to a previous state', so 'revert back' is redundant, and this phrase feels unclear to international readers expecting 'reply' or 'respond'. Replace 'revert back' with 'reply' or 'get back to' for clearer international communication.

commonMistakes.wrong: I am not knowing the answer.
commonMistakes.right: I don't know the answer.
commonMistakes.why: 'Know' is a state verb and should never appear in continuous form - this is one of the most frequent patterns in Indian English influenced by regional language structures. Practice using simple present ('don't know') instead of the continuous form for all state verbs.

commonMistakes.wrong: Kindly do one thing.
commonMistakes.right: Could you please... / Please...
commonMistakes.why: "Kindly do one thing" is a very common introductory phrase in Indian English, but it sounds unusual and overly formal to international readers - simply state your request directly with 'please' or 'could you'. Simplify introductory request phrases for clearer, more natural-sounding international English.

commonMistakes.wrong: Myself Raj, I am working in IT.
commonMistakes.right: My name is Raj, and I work in IT. / I am Raj, and I work in IT.
commonMistakes.why: Starting an introduction with "Myself [name]" is common in Indian English but isn't standard grammatically - 'myself' is a reflexive pronoun, not meant to introduce yourself this way. Use 'My name is...' or 'I am...' as the standard way to introduce yourself in English.

commonMistakes.wrong: What is your good name?
commonMistakes.right: What is your name?
commonMistakes.why: "Good name" is a direct translation from Hindi and other Indian languages, but in English, simply 'name' is used without 'good' in front. Ask simply 'What is your name?' - the word 'good' isn't needed or standard in English.

commonMistakes.wrong: I passed out from college in 2015.
commonMistakes.right: I graduated from college in 2015.
commonMistakes.why: "Passed out" in international English actually means 'fainted' or 'lost consciousness' - the term for completing your studies is 'graduated'. Always use 'graduate/graduated' to describe completing an educational program, never 'passed out'.

commonMistakes.wrong: Please open the light.
commonMistakes.right: Please turn on the light.
commonMistakes.why: "Open the light" is a direct translation pattern from Hindi and other languages, but in English, lights and electronic devices are 'turned on/off', not 'opened/closed'. Use 'turn on/off' specifically for lights, fans, and electronic devices.

commonMistakes.wrong: Please close the light.
commonMistakes.right: Please turn off the light.
commonMistakes.why: Just like the previous example, this should use 'turn off' rather than 'close' for lights and similar devices. Keep grouping these together: turn on/off, not open/close, for lights and electronics.

commonMistakes.wrong: I will do it in two-two days.
commonMistakes.right: I will do it in a couple of days. / I will do it in two days.
commonMistakes.why: Repeating a number ("two-two") for emphasis is a pattern from Indian languages, but standard English doesn't repeat numbers this way - simply state the number once. Say the number just once, or use 'a couple of' to mean approximately two.

commonMistakes.wrong: He is doing timepass.
commonMistakes.right: He is relaxing. / He is passing the time.
commonMistakes.why: "Timepass" is a term commonly used in Indian English (borrowed and adapted into a single word) but isn't standard international English - use 'relaxing', 'passing the time', or 'killing time' instead. Replace uniquely Indian English terms with their standard international equivalents for clearer global communication.

commonMistakes.wrong: I stay in Mumbai.
commonMistakes.right: I live in Mumbai.
commonMistakes.why: In Indian English, 'stay' is often used to mean 'live' permanently, but in international English, 'stay' usually means a temporary visit (like staying at a hotel), while 'live' means your permanent residence. Use 'live' for where you permanently reside, and reserve 'stay' for temporary visits.

commonMistakes.wrong: She is my cousin brother.
commonMistakes.right: He is my cousin. / She is my cousin.
commonMistakes.why: "Cousin brother" and "cousin sister" are phrases used in Indian English to specify gender, but in standard English, 'cousin' alone doesn't indicate gender, and adding 'brother/sister' is considered nonstandard. Simply say 'cousin' without adding 'brother' or 'sister' - English doesn't distinguish cousin gender this way.

commonMistakes.wrong: I have a doubt.
commonMistakes.right: I have a question.
commonMistakes.why: In Indian English, 'doubt' is commonly used to mean 'question', but in international English, 'doubt' specifically means uncertainty or disbelief about something, not simply a question you want to ask. Use 'question' when you want to ask something, and reserve 'doubt' for genuine uncertainty or skepticism.

commonMistakes.wrong: Please give one missed call.
commonMistakes.right: Please give me a call. / Please call me and hang up.
commonMistakes.why: "Missed call" as a verb phrase ("give a missed call") is a uniquely Indian English usage describing calling briefly and hanging up before it's answered - this specific practice and phrase isn't widely understood internationally. If explaining this practice to an international audience, describe it fully rather than assuming the phrase is understood.

commonMistakes.wrong: I am understanding the concept now.
commonMistakes.right: I understand the concept now.
commonMistakes.why: 'Understand' is a state verb, so it should stay in simple present, not continuous form. This exact mistake pattern (state verbs in continuous form) appears extremely frequently in Indian English, so it's worth mastering thoroughly.

commonMistakes.wrong: He is having a lot of tension.
commonMistakes.right: He is very stressed. / He has a lot of stress.
commonMistakes.why: In Indian English, 'tension' is often used to mean 'stress' or 'worry', but in international English, 'tension' more specifically refers to physical or interpersonal strain (tension in a rope, tension between two people). Use 'stress' or 'worry' to describe someone's mental/emotional state, saving 'tension' for its more specific meanings.

commonMistakes.wrong: Don't take tension.
commonMistakes.right: Don't worry. / Don't stress.
commonMistakes.why: Just like the previous example, 'tension' here should be replaced with 'worry' or 'stress' for clearer, more standard English. Practice this specific phrase correction since it's extremely common in everyday Indian English conversation.

commonMistakes.wrong: I am agreeing with you.
commonMistakes.right: I agree with you.
commonMistakes.why: 'Agree' is a regular verb expressing an opinion or state, and it's not typically used in continuous form when stating agreement. Stick with simple present ('I agree') rather than continuous form for expressing opinions and agreement.

commonMistakes.wrong: Prepone the meeting to Monday.
commonMistakes.right: Move the meeting earlier to Monday. / Reschedule the meeting to an earlier date.
commonMistakes.why: "Prepone" (the opposite of 'postpone') is a word used in Indian English but isn't standard or widely recognized in international English - use 'move earlier' or 'reschedule' instead. While logically clever, 'prepone' should be replaced with standard phrasing for international communication.

commonMistakes.wrong: He is out of station.
commonMistakes.right: He is out of town. / He is away.
commonMistakes.why: "Out of station" is a phrase from Indian English (originally from railway terminology) meaning someone is traveling or away - international English uses 'out of town' or simply 'away'. Replace 'out of station' with 'out of town' for clearer communication with international audiences.

commonMistakes.wrong: I will be reaching by 5 PM.
commonMistakes.right: I will arrive by 5 PM. / I will reach there by 5 PM (also acceptable, but 'arrive' is more standard).
commonMistakes.why: While 'reach' can be used this way, 'arrive' is the more standard, widely recognized verb for this meaning in international English. Consider using 'arrive' as your primary choice, especially in formal writing, though 'reach' is understood too.

commonMistakes.wrong: Please share your good self's feedback.
commonMistakes.right: Please share your feedback.
commonMistakes.why: "Good self" is an overly formal, indirect way of saying 'you' that's specific to certain Indian English business writing traditions, but international English simply uses 'you' or 'your' directly. Simplify formal, indirect phrases like 'your good self' to the direct 'you' for clearer modern business writing.

commonMistakes.wrong: I am a fresher, looking for my first job.
commonMistakes.right: I am a recent graduate, looking for my first job. / I am new to the workforce.
commonMistakes.why: "Fresher" is commonly used in Indian English (and Indian job markets) to mean someone new to the workforce, but international English typically uses 'recent graduate' or 'entry-level candidate'. Use 'recent graduate' or 'entry-level' in international resumes and job applications instead of 'fresher'.

commonMistakes.wrong: He is a good boy, no?
commonMistakes.right: He is a good boy, isn't he?
commonMistakes.why: Ending a statement with "no?" as a general question tag is common in some Indian English speech patterns, but standard English uses a proper question tag matching the sentence's verb (isn't he, doesn't he, can't he). Use the correctly matched question tag based on the main verb, rather than a generic "no?" or "isn't it?" for every sentence.

commonMistakes.wrong: This is very much correct, isn't it?
commonMistakes.right: This is quite correct, isn't it? / This is absolutely correct, isn't it?
commonMistakes.why: "Very much correct" is a slightly unusual intensifier combination in Indian English - 'quite', 'absolutely', or simply 'very' work better as standard intensifiers before 'correct'. Choose standard intensifiers (very, quite, absolutely) rather than combining 'very much' before an adjective like this.

commonMistakes.wrong: I have completed my graduation.
commonMistakes.right: I have completed my degree. / I have graduated.
commonMistakes.why: In Indian English, 'graduation' sometimes refers to the degree itself (bachelor's degree), while in international English, 'graduation' specifically means the ceremony or event of completing your studies. Use 'degree' to refer to your qualification, and 'graduation' only for the ceremony or moment of completing it.

commonMistakes.wrong: She is expert in cooking.
commonMistakes.right: She is an expert in cooking. / She is skilled at cooking.
commonMistakes.why: This is missing the article 'an' before 'expert' - since 'expert' is a singular countable noun here, it needs an article. Remember to always include the correct article (a/an) before singular countable nouns like 'expert', 'teacher', or 'doctor'.

commonMistakes.wrong: I got a cold since yesterday.
commonMistakes.right: I have had a cold since yesterday.
commonMistakes.why: Since this describes something starting in the past and continuing to now (marked by 'since'), it needs present perfect ('have had'), not simple past ('got'). Whenever 'since' appears with a starting point, use present perfect tense, not simple past.

commonMistakes.wrong: Please do the needful and revert back at the earliest.
commonMistakes.right: Please take the necessary action and reply as soon as possible.
commonMistakes.why: This sentence combines two commonly criticized Indian English business phrases together - both "do the needful" and "revert back" should be replaced with clearer, more direct international English. Avoid stacking multiple old-fashioned formal phrases together; use direct, clear language instead.

commonMistakes.wrong: I stay with my family in Delhi.
commonMistakes.right: I live with my family in Delhi.
commonMistakes.why: This repeats the stay/live distinction covered earlier - since this describes a permanent living situation, 'live' is the correct choice, not 'stay'. Keep reinforcing this distinction: live = permanent residence, stay = temporary visit.

commonMistakes.wrong: He is a nice person, actually.
commonMistakes.right: He is a nice person.
commonMistakes.why: While not incorrect, the word "actually" is used as a filler word extremely frequently in Indian English speech, often without adding real meaning - consider whether it's truly needed in each sentence. Use 'actually' sparingly and only when genuinely contrasting with an expectation, rather than as a habitual filler word.

commonMistakes.wrong: I am having a headache since morning.
commonMistakes.right: I have had a headache since morning. / I have a headache.
commonMistakes.why: This combines two mistakes: 'have' for physical symptoms shouldn't be in continuous form, and if using 'since', the tense should be present perfect ('have had'), not present continuous. Physical states and symptoms (headache, fever, pain) use simple 'have' or present perfect 'have had', never continuous form.

commonMistakes.wrong: Give me one minute, I am coming.
commonMistakes.right: Give me a minute, I'm coming. / One moment, please.
commonMistakes.why: While understandable, "one minute" is slightly unusual phrasing compared to the more standard 'a minute' or 'a moment' in international English. Prefer 'a minute/moment' over 'one minute' for smoother, more natural-sounding English.

commonMistakes.wrong: I need to purchase some grocery.
commonMistakes.right: I need to buy some groceries.
commonMistakes.why: 'Grocery' items are referred to as 'groceries' (plural) in standard English, and 'buy' is more commonly used in everyday speech than the more formal 'purchase'. Use 'groceries' (plural) for shopping items, and 'buy' for everyday casual speech.

commonMistakes.wrong: This is a hi-fi restaurant.
commonMistakes.right: This is a fancy/upscale restaurant.
commonMistakes.why: "Hi-fi" (meaning 'high quality' or 'fancy') is used informally in Indian English but isn't standard international usage - 'hi-fi' internationally refers only to high-fidelity audio equipment. Replace 'hi-fi' meaning 'fancy' with 'upscale', 'fancy', or 'high-end' for international clarity.

commonMistakes.wrong: He is a very good, nice, sincere, hardworking boy.
commonMistakes.right: He is a hardworking and sincere person.
commonMistakes.why: While not strictly wrong, stacking many similar adjectives together (good, nice, sincere, hardworking) is a common style in Indian English descriptions, but international writing tends to prefer 1-2 well-chosen, specific descriptive words. Aim for fewer, more precise adjectives rather than long lists of similar-meaning words.

commonMistakes.wrong: I completed my schooling from Delhi Public School.
commonMistakes.right: I completed my schooling at Delhi Public School.
commonMistakes.why: The preposition 'at' is used with specific institution names (school, university), not 'from' - 'from' is reserved for describing where you graduated or the source of something. Use 'at' when referring to studying at a specific institution, and 'from' when describing where you graduated.

commonMistakes.wrong: I am not able to do this work.
commonMistakes.right: I can't do this work. / I'm unable to do this work.
commonMistakes.why: While grammatically correct, "not able to" is a longer, less natural way of expressing inability compared to the more common 'can't' or 'unable to'. Prefer the more concise, natural 'can't' or 'unable to' over the longer 'not able to' in everyday speech and writing.

commonMistakes.wrong: Kindly revert with your valuable feedback.
commonMistakes.right: Please share your feedback.
commonMistakes.why: This combines two commonly flagged Indian business English phrases: "kindly revert" and "valuable feedback" - simplify to direct, natural phrasing. Avoid stacking multiple formal, indirect phrases together in professional communication; state your request plainly and directly.

commonMistakes.wrong: I am eagerly waiting for your positive reply.
commonMistakes.right: I look forward to your reply. / I hope to hear from you soon.
commonMistakes.why: While understandable, this phrase is a very formal, somewhat old-fashioned pattern common in Indian English correspondence - modern international business writing prefers shorter, more direct closings. Use simpler, more modern closing phrases like 'I look forward to your reply' in professional emails.

commonMistakes.wrong: This is not up to the mark.
commonMistakes.right: This isn't up to standard. / This doesn't meet expectations.
commonMistakes.why: "Up to the mark" is understood in some varieties of English but 'up to standard' or 'meets expectations' are more universally recognized alternatives in international business contexts. Consider using more globally recognized phrases in professional writing meant for diverse, international audiences.

commonMistakes.wrong: I want to become an engineer, so that I can serve the society.
commonMistakes.right: I want to become an engineer so that I can serve society.
commonMistakes.why: In this general sense (society as a whole concept), 'society' doesn't need 'the' in front of it - this is similar to how we don't say 'the life' for life in general. Drop 'the' when referring to society, life, or nature as broad, general concepts rather than one specific instance.

commonMistakes.wrong: We are like family only.
commonMistakes.right: We are like family.
commonMistakes.why: Adding "only" at the end for emphasis is a common pattern borrowed from Indian languages, but it's not needed in standard English and can sound repetitive or unclear to international readers. Simply end the sentence without 'only' when it's not logically limiting anything specific.

commonMistakes.wrong: I have two-three items to discuss.
commonMistakes.right: I have two or three items to discuss. / I have a few items to discuss.
commonMistakes.why: Similar to "two-two days", combining two numbers with a hyphen ("two-three") is a pattern from Indian languages - standard English uses 'or' between the numbers, or simply 'a few'. Use 'two or three' or 'a few' instead of hyphenating two numbers together.

commonMistakes.wrong: Please find the attachment herewith.
commonMistakes.right: Please find the attachment attached. / I've attached the file.
commonMistakes.why: "Herewith" is a very formal, old-fashioned word rarely used in modern international business English - simpler, more natural phrasing is preferred today. Replace old-fashioned formal words like 'herewith' with simpler modern equivalents in everyday professional writing.

commonMistakes.wrong: I am understanding your concern.
commonMistakes.right: I understand your concern.
commonMistakes.why: This repeats the classic state-verb continuous mistake one final time - 'understand' should always stay in simple present, never continuous form. By now, this pattern should feel completely automatic: state verbs (know, understand, want, like, believe) never take '-ing'.

commonMistakes.wrong: He is having good English.
commonMistakes.right: He has good English. / He speaks English well.
commonMistakes.why: 'Have' describing a skill or quality is a state, not an action, so it stays in simple present ('has'), never continuous ('is having'). This is the same core state-verb rule applied once more to a skill description rather than a physical possession.

commonMistakes.wrong: What is the timing of the shop?
commonMistakes.right: What are the shop's hours? / When does the shop open and close?
commonMistakes.why: "Timing" (singular) used this way to ask about opening hours is common in Indian English, but international English uses 'hours' (plural) or asks directly about opening/closing times. Use 'hours' or ask directly about opening and closing times instead of 'timing' for this meaning.

quiz.question: Choose the internationally standard sentence:
quiz.explanation: 'Have' showing possession/family is a state verb and should stay in simple present, not continuous form.

quiz.sentence: Revert back to me by tomorrow.
quiz.correction: Please get back to me by tomorrow.
quiz.explanation: 'Revert' already means 'return to a previous state', so 'revert back' is redundant; 'get back to' is clearer internationally.

quiz.question: Choose the internationally standard sentence:
options: I am staying in Mumbai permanently.
quiz.explanation: 'Live' is used for permanent residence; 'stay' typically implies something temporary in international English.

quiz.question: Please turn ___ the light. (not 'open')
quiz.explanation: Lights and electronic devices are 'turned on/off' in English, not 'opened/closed'.

quiz.question: Choose the internationally standard way to introduce yourself:
quiz.explanation: 'Myself' is a reflexive pronoun and isn't standard for introductions; use 'My name is...' or 'I am...'.
